event: Early Archaic (pre-ceramic) hunter-gatherer and fishing peoples settle the islands of Guadeloupe, leaving the earliest known traces of human presence.
Arawak-speaking agriculturalists migrating from the Orinoco basin in South America establish ceramic Saladoid culture villages across the archipelago.
Saladoid communities flourish, cultivating cassava and producing distinctive painted pottery, with rock carvings (petroglyphs) left at sites such as the Trois-Rivières.
The later Troumassoid and Suazey cultures develop as Arawak society on the islands evolves over the centuries.
Carib (Kalinago) peoples, having migrated north, dominate the islands they call Karukera ('island of beautiful waters').
Christopher Columbus sights and lands on the island during his second voyage, naming it Santa Maria de Guadalupe after a Spanish monastery.
An early French attempt to settle the island fails in the face of resistance from the Carib inhabitants.
Charles Liénard de l'Olive and Jean du Plessis d'Ossonville claim Guadeloupe for the French Compagnie des Îles de l'Amérique, beginning sustained French colonization.
Sugar cane cultivation is introduced, laying the foundation for a plantation economy worked by enslaved Africans.
A treaty between the French, English and Caribs nominally protects surviving Carib communities, though indigenous presence continues to dwindle.
Guadeloupe becomes a direct possession of the French Crown, ending company rule and tightening royal administration.
France's Code Noir is promulgated, codifying the harsh legal regime governing enslaved Africans across its Caribbean colonies including Guadeloupe.
British forces capture Guadeloupe during the Seven Years' War, occupying the island and expanding its sugar trade.
The Treaty of Paris returns Guadeloupe to France, with Britain choosing to retain Canada instead of the prosperous sugar island.
News of the French Revolution reaches Guadeloupe, stirring political tensions between royalists, republicans, free people of color, and the enslaved.
Victor Hugues retakes the island from the British and enforces the National Convention's abolition of slavery, arming freed people against royalist planters.
Napoleon Bonaparte's forces reimpose slavery; Louis Delgrès leads a doomed rebellion, dying with his followers in a defiant explosion at Matouba.
British forces again seize Guadeloupe during the Napoleonic Wars.
Guadeloupe is briefly ceded to Sweden under the Treaty of Stockholm before reverting to British control.
Guadeloupe is definitively restored to France following the final defeat of Napoleon.
Slavery is permanently abolished in Guadeloupe through the efforts of Victor Schoelcher, freeing tens of thousands of enslaved people.
Indentured laborers from India begin arriving to work the sugar plantations, reshaping the island's demographics and culture.
Citizens of Guadeloupe gain the right to elect representatives to the French National Assembly under the Third Republic.
A devastating fire and economic hardship strike Pointe-à-Pitre amid the decline of the sugar economy.
A catastrophic hurricane devastates Guadeloupe, killing over a thousand people and ruining much of the agricultural economy.
Guadeloupe is transformed from a colony into an overseas department (département d'outre-mer) of France, granting full French citizenship to its people.
Guadeloupeans vote to remain within the French Republic under Charles de Gaulle's new Fifth Republic constitution.
Deadly clashes in Pointe-à-Pitre during a labor and pro-independence protest leave numerous demonstrators killed by French security forces.
The La Soufrière volcano erupts, prompting the evacuation of around 70,000 residents from the Basse-Terre region.
An autonomist and independence movement, at times marked by bombings, intensifies pressure against the French administration.
France's decentralization laws give Guadeloupe an elected regional council alongside its departmental institutions.
Hurricane Hugo strikes Guadeloupe, causing widespread destruction and severe damage to homes and crops.
Guadeloupe voters reject a referendum that would have merged the departmental and regional councils into a single authority.
Saint-Barthélemy and Saint-Martin separate from Guadeloupe to become distinct French overseas collectivities.
A 44-day general strike led by the LKP collective paralyzes the island in protest against the high cost of living and economic inequality.
The ACTe Memorial, a major museum dedicated to slavery and the slave trade, opens in Pointe-à-Pitre.
Hurricanes Irma and Maria pass near or strike the region, with Guadeloupe serving as a relief hub for devastated neighboring islands.
Protests against COVID-19 vaccine mandates and longstanding social grievances erupt into unrest and looting across the island.
